era_context: Thutmose III's imperial consolidation in the Levant was well established, with Egyptian hegemony firmly entrenched. Hatshepsut's power was at or near its peak. The Mycenaean civilization continued to flourish in the Aegean, with palace centers at their height. The Shang dynasty was in the middle of its reign in China. In South Asia, the Vedic period was underway.

events.title: Thutmose III's Continued Levantine Campaigns and Tribute Collection
events.description: Thutmose III continued his annual campaigns to maintain Egyptian control over the Levant and to collect tribute from subject city-states. These campaigns were characterized by rapid military movements, the use of chariotry, and coordinated logistics. The campaigns served to suppress any potential rebellions and to maintain Egyptian military superiority in the region. The tribute collected from these campaigns was substantial and contributed significantly to Egyptian wealth.

sources.name: Thutmose III's Annals at Karnak Temple

events.certainty_note: Thutmose III's annals record multiple campaigns, but the precise chronology and dating of individual campaigns is debated. The campaigns are generally dated to the early years of his reign, but exact year-by-year assignments are uncertain by ±2–3 years.
cross_references: Part of broader Thutmosid imperial expansion

events.title: Hatshepsut's Mortuary Temple Construction at Deir el-Bahari
events.description: Hatshepsut's mortuary temple at Deir el-Bahari was under construction during this period. The temple is one of the most impressive monuments of ancient Egypt, featuring terraced colonnades, elaborate reliefs, and inscriptions. The construction of such a monument required significant resources, skilled labor, and architectural innovation. The temple served as a vehicle for Hatshepsut's self-promotion and the legitimation of her rule, and it remains one of the most iconic structures of ancient Egypt.

sources.name: The Temple of Deir el-Bahari (archaeological evidence and inscriptions)

events.certainty_note: The construction of Hatshepsut's mortuary temple is well-attested through archaeological evidence and inscriptions. The temple was built over a period of years, and the exact dates of construction phases are debated, but the temple was largely completed by the end of Hatshepsut's reign (c. 1458 BCE).
cross_references: Part of Hatshepsut's consolidation of power and self-promotion

events.title: Mycenaean Palace Administration and Linear B Record-Keeping
events.description: The Mycenaean palace centers at Mycenae, Tiryns, and Pylos were engaged in sophisticated administrative and economic activities, as documented in Linear B tablets. These tablets record information about taxation, labor organization, military equipment, religious offerings, and trade. The palace-centered economy was highly organized and efficient, with centralized control over resources and production. The Linear B tablets provide a detailed picture of Mycenaean society and economy during this period.
key_figures: Mycenaean rulers and administrators (names not preserved in contemporary records)

sources.name: Linear B tablets from Knossos, Pylos, and Mycenae

events.certainty_note: The existence of Mycenaean palace administration and record-keeping is well-attested through Linear B tablets. The dating is based on stratigraphic and ceramic evidence, with an uncertainty of ±10–20 years.
cross_references: Contemporary with Thutmose III's reign in Egypt and Hatshepsut's building program

events.title: Shang Dynasty Continued Rule in China
events.description: The Shang dynasty continued its rule over the Yellow River Valley and surrounding regions during this period. The Shang were known for their bronze-working technology, oracle bone divination practices, and centralized state organization. The Shang capital was located at Anyang, and the dynasty maintained control through a combination of military force and religious authority. The Shang period is characterized by the development of early Chinese writing and the establishment of many cultural practices that would persist in later Chinese civilization.
key_figures: Shang rulers (specific names uncertain for this period)

sources.name: Archaeological evidence from Anyang and other Shang sites
Later Chinese historical texts (Shiji, Bamboo Annals)

events.certainty_note: The Shang dynasty's continued rule during this period is attested through oracle bone inscriptions and archaeological evidence. However, the absolute dating of the Shang dynasty is debated, with different chronologies placing the Shang period at different dates. The conventional dating places the Shang dynasty from c. 1600 to c. 1046 BCE, but alternative chronologies place it later. The dating uncertainty is ±50–100 years.
cross_references: Contemporary with Thutmose III's reign in Egypt

disconfirming_evidence: The extent of Egyptian control over the Levant may have been less comprehensive than Egyptian inscriptions suggest. Some scholars argue that local rulers maintained more autonomy than official records indicate. Additionally, the dating of Hatshepsut's building projects is debated, with some scholars placing the major construction of her mortuary temple later in her reign. The absolute dating of the Shang dynasty is highly debated, with different chronologies placing the Shang period at significantly different dates. Some scholars place the Shang dynasty 50–100 years later than the conventional dating used here.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 1475 BCE is dominated by Egyptian sources, particularly the annals of Thutmose III and inscriptions related to Hatshepsut. The Mycenaean world is known primarily through archaeological evidence and Linear B tablets. The Shang dynasty is known through oracle bone inscriptions and archaeological evidence, but the absolute dating is debated. The perspectives of Levantine populations, Mycenaean commoners, and most of the Shang population are largely absent from the surviving record. The Egyptian record is shaped by royal propaganda and may overstate the extent of Egyptian control and military success.

graph_edges.note: Continued campaigns to consolidate and maintain Egyptian control in the Levant
Hatshepsut's building program continued throughout her reign
Mycenaean palace administration continued throughout the Late Bronze Age II
